name: Utah
examName: Utah Real Estate Sales Agent Examination

examFacts.notes: Administered by Pearson VUE for the Utah Division of Real Estate. The 80-question national portion and the 50-question Utah state-law portion are scored separately, each requiring a scaled score of 70.

intro:
Utah's real estate exam carries a 50-question state section scored on its own, and it's where first-time candidates most often slip. The Utah Division of Real Estate delivers it through Pearson VUE as two separate portions, an 80-question national section and a 50-question Utah section, for 130 scored questions across four hours of testing time. You need a scaled score of 70 on each portion and the two are scored separately, so a strong national score won't rescue a weak state score. That is why Utah-specific preparation matters more here than another pass through national review.

State-specific prep matters more here than almost anywhere else. The Utah section leans on the Real Estate Licensing and Practice Act and Division rules, the limited agency relationship Utah uses in place of dual agency, the state-approved Real Estate Purchase Contract a sales agent fills in rather than drafts, trust account and earnest money handling, the Real Estate Education Research and Recovery Fund, and special Utah topics like prior appropriation water rights and the Lien Recovery Fund. None of that looks like the material a candidate in a neighboring state studies. A generic real estate practice test won't cut it. You need Utah-specific practice questions.

Below are 20 free Utah real estate test questions and answers from RealReady's full Utah bank. Each tests a specific topic with the correct answer and a plain-English explanation under the card. Read each one, commit to an answer, then reveal. Treat it like a real Utah Division of Real Estate practice test, and the ones you miss show you exactly what to focus on next.

Get the RealReady app on iPhone, iPad, or Android for the rest of the Utah question bank. The full app includes short articles that walk you through the why behind each topic, detailed explanations on every question, a missed-question drill mode, and progress tracking that shows your per-category accuracy.

faq.q: How many questions are on the Utah real estate exam?
faq.a: The Utah Real Estate Sales Agent Examination has 130 scored questions, split into an 80-question national portion and a 50-question Utah state portion. Pearson VUE also mixes in unscored pretest questions, 5 on the national portion and 5 to 10 on the state portion, that don't count toward your result. You get four hours in total to finish both portions.

faq.q: What's the passing score for the Utah real estate exam?
faq.a: Utah uses a scaled score that runs from 0 to 100, and you need a 70 on each of the two portions, the national and the Utah state portion, scored separately. The scaled score is not the raw count or percentage of questions you got right, it is an equated score that adjusts for small differences in difficulty between exam forms. Because the portions are scored separately, a strong score on one won't offset a low score on the other. If you pass one portion and fail the other, you only retake the failed portion within six months.

faq.q: How much does the Utah real estate exam cost?
faq.a: Pearson VUE charges $69 to sit the Utah sales agent exam, paid by card or voucher when you make your reservation. The fee is non-refundable and non-transferable, and you pay it again for each examination reservation, including a retake of a single portion. Sales agent is the entry-level real estate license Utah issues.

faq.q: How long is the Utah real estate exam?
faq.a: Four hours in total. Pearson VUE runs the national and Utah portions in a single four-hour session, and the exam ends automatically when your time expires. You report to the test center about 30 minutes early and leave with an official pass or fail score report in hand.

faq.q: Is the Utah real estate exam hard?
faq.a: Plenty of first-time candidates pass, but the exam earns its reputation and the Utah state portion is where people stumble. The 80-question national portion is manageable for anyone who actually studied general principles. The 50-question state portion catches people off guard, because Utah's limited agency model, the state-approved Real Estate Purchase Contract, trust account rules, and topics like prior appropriation water rights and the Lien Recovery Fund don't match what national prep material teaches. Honest practice on Utah-specific questions is usually the difference between passing the first time and paying to retake a portion.

faq.q: What's on the Utah real estate exam?
faq.a: The 80-question national portion covers general real estate principles, property characteristics and ownership, valuation and appraisal, contracts and agency, real estate practice, disclosures and environmental issues, financing and settlement, and real estate math. The 50-question Utah portion focuses on the Division of Real Estate and the Real Estate Commission, licensing and license maintenance, office procedures and trust accounts, licensee practice and approved forms, agency including limited agency, enforcement, the Real Estate Education Research and Recovery Fund, and special Utah topics like water rights, the Lien Recovery Fund, and closing statements. The Utah material carries more weight than its share of questions suggests, because it is the part most candidates have never seen before.

faq.q: What's the best way to prepare for the Utah real estate exam?
faq.a: After you finish your 120 hours of sales agent prelicensing education, the highest-leverage move is grinding through Utah-specific practice questions with honest feedback on every miss. National practice tests help with the 80 national questions but won't catch you on Utah's limited agency rules, approved forms, and trust account requirements. The RealReady app gives you the full Utah question bank, plus a missed-question mode and per-category progress tracking so you can drill your weak spots. Most users study 30 to 60 minutes a day for two to four weeks before sitting the exam.
